module_map.responsibility: Core SQL parsing engine that converts token stream to AST. Uses recursive descent parsing. Implements 425+ methods for parsing SQL constructs. Dialect-specific parsers extend this base.
Base AST node definitions. Contains Expression base class and tree manipulation methods (walk, find, transform). Defines 111 classes covering query nodes, predicates, and operators.
String function expression classes (104 classes). Includes operations: Concat, Replace, Substring, Length, Soundex, Levenshtein, etc. Defines SQL semantics for string operations.
AST to SQL text generation. Implements 454 methods mapping expression types to SQL output. Dialect-aware: generates syntax matching target dialect. Handles formatting and identifier quoting.
Dialect hub: defines base Dialect class (140 methods, 4 classes) that coordinates tokenization, parsing, and generation. Registry of 30+ SQL dialects (postgres, snowflake, duckdb, bigquery, etc.).
Query optimization passes (21 modules, 308 functions). Stages: annotate_types, qualify_columns, pushdown_predicates, simplify, eliminate_joins. Traverses and rewrites AST for efficiency.
In-memory SQL execution engine. Interprets optimized AST on Python data structures. Modules: python.py (expression evaluation), table.py (table abstraction), env.py (runtime context).
Token type enumeration and Token data class. Defines TokenType enum (100+ values) for all SQL keywords/operators. Tokenizer produces Token stream as input to parser.
Tokenization logic. Converts SQL string to Token stream via lexer patterns. Handles keywords, operators, literals, identifiers. 3 classes, 26 functions. Used by dialect tokenizers.

pipeline_trace.key_function: Parser.parse(tokens) → Parser._parse() → recursive descent methods (_parse_expression, _parse_function, etc.)
Expression.__init__(), Func subclasses define arg_types and _sql_names
Optimizer.optimize(ast, schema) → apply passes: annotate_types, qualify_columns, pushdown_predicates, simplify, etc.
Executor.execute(ast, tables) → interpret Expression tree recursively, evaluate functions
Generator.generate(ast) → recursive dispatch on expression type → sql_<classname>() methods → SQL string

cross_module_hubs.reason: Central AST node definition. All submodules (parser, optimizer, executor, generator, dialects) import Expression base class. Defines tree traversal (walk, find, transform) used globally.
Dialect registry and factory. Entry point for parse(), tokenize(), generate() APIs. Coordinates with parser, generator, tokenizer for all 30+ dialects. Each dialect imports Dialect base class.
Parser core used by all dialect parsers (parsers/*.py inherit Parser class). Imports all expression types. Referenced by dialect.py for parsing coordination.

files_to_modify: /tmp/sqlglot/sqlglot/expressions/string.py (ALREADY DEFINED: Levenshtein class exists at line 74-82)
/tmp/sqlglot/sqlglot/parser.py (add parser logic if dialect-specific syntax needed)
/tmp/sqlglot/sqlglot/generator.py (add Levenshtein SQL generation if not already present)
new_types: Levenshtein (already exists in expressions/string.py as Expression subclass with arg_types: this, expression, ins_cost, del_cost, sub_cost, max_dist)
change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: String function pattern from existing implementations (Soundex, EditDistance). 1) Define Expression subclass in expressions/string.py with arg_types dict and optional _sql_names. 2) Add parser rule in parser.py if custom syntax needed. 3) Add generator method in generator.py: sql_levenshtein() that renders args. 4) Override in dialect-specific generators if syntax differs (e.g., snowflake may have different arg order).
change_proposal.integration_point: expressions/string.py line 74 already defines Levenshtein with proper arg structure. Integration points: (1) Parser recognizes LEVENSHTEIN keyword → maps to Levenshtein() constructor. (2) Generator.sql_levenshtein() method generates output. (3) Optimizer/Executor can treat as regular function call. Dialect-specific tweaks in dialects/*.py if needed.
risks: Levenshtein already defined: verify existing implementation matches target database semantics (arg count, cost parameters, max_dist behavior vary across vendors).
Parser coverage: ensure all dialects' parsers recognize LEVENSHTEIN keyword. Some dialects may use different names (e.g., EDIT_DISTANCE, DISTANCE).
Generator output: verify generated SQL matches target dialect. DuckDB uses LEVENSHTEIN(str1, str2); Snowflake uses EDITDISTANCE(str1, str2); PostgreSQL uses LEVENSHTEIN().
Type coercion: ensure string arguments are properly coerced/validated at parse time or runtime.
No standardization: different dialects have different signatures (2-6 args), return types, cost parameters. Must test per-dialect.
